title: Diffusion-Based Planning for Autonomous Driving with Flexible Guidance

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Diffusion Planner, a transformer-based diffusion model for closed-loop motion planning that jointly generates the ego trajectory and neighboring agents’ futures. The method aims to address multi-modality, reduce reliance on rule-based refinement, and enable flexible, training-free behavioral adaptation via classifier-style guidance based on differentiable energy functions. On the nuPlan benchmark and a newly collected 200-hour delivery-vehicle dataset, the method achieves state-of-the-art results among learning-based baselines without post-processing and further improves to match or surpass hybrid/rule-based systems when an existing refinement module is attached.
strengths: Leverages a DiT-style architecture to jointly model multi-agent futures (planning + prediction) as one diffusion target, mitigating exposure bias and capturing interactions.
Introduces practical conditioning and fusion schemes (MLP-Mixer for polylines, AdaLN for navigation+timestep) that keep the architecture simple yet effective.

Demonstrates training-free classifier guidance via diffusion posterior sampling for controllable planning (speed, comfort, collision/drivable constraints), showing meaningful runtime behavior shaping without retraining.

Strong closed-loop evaluations on nuPlan across Val14, Test14, and Test14-hard, in both non-reactive and reactive modes; competitive to or surpassing strong rule-based/hybrid baselines once refinement is added.
Additional evaluation on a 200-hour delivery-vehicle dataset highlighting cross-domain transfer to more conservative driving and bike-lane scenarios.
Ablations on data processing, ego-state handling, number of predicted neighbors, and inference hyperparameters provide insight into key design choices.

The problem setup, diffusion formulation (x0 prediction), and conditioning mechanisms are described clearly with an intuitive architectural diagram and equations that align with standard score-based modeling.
The guidance mechanism is well-motivated and illustrated with qualitative case studies showing composability.
Demonstrates that, with appropriate architectural design and conditioning, diffusion can deliver high-quality closed-loop planning without heavy rule-based post-processing.
The training-free guidance capability is practically attractive for deployment where post-training adjustments are desired.
weaknesses: No formal safety guarantees despite the “safe” guidance framing; the energies are heuristic and require tuning, and their effect on tail safety distributions is not systematically quantified.

Joint modeling is central, but there is limited ablation that isolates the benefit of joint diffusion vs. separate prediction-then-planning or single-agent variants beyond varying the number of neighbors M.
Limited quantitative evaluation of the guidance mechanism’s impact on aggregate closed-loop metrics (e.g., collision rate, off-road, comfort) across a sweep of guidance strengths and combinations.
Real-time claims (≈20 Hz) are promising but lack a thorough latency breakdown, hardware specs, and comparisons to baselines under matched conditions.

Post-processing still provides further gains and is used to surpass hybrid/rule-based baselines; the claim of not relying on rules would be stronger with a more comprehensive head-to-head against hybrids without any refinement.

Some implementation details (e.g., exact guidance weights, stability/robustness of guidance across scenarios) are deferred to the appendix; summarizing key values and sensitivities in the main text would help.
The distinction between “not relying on rules” and using rule-like differentiable energy functions at inference could be clarified to avoid overclaiming.

Recent joint prediction–planning diffusion frameworks (e.g., JPPD/CDiT with differentiable safety guidance and flow matching) are not discussed or compared. A conceptual and empirical comparison would strengthen positioning.
A broader quantitative comparison to other diffusion planning approaches (beyond those listed in Appendix B.1) and to classifier-free–style guidance variants would be informative.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide a quantitative study of the guidance mechanism across a range of weights (e.g., collision/drivable/comfort) and report its net effect on nuPlan closed-loop safety metrics (collision rate, off-road, TTC) and comfort?
2. How sensitive is the system to guidance weight tuning across scenarios? Do you propose any default schedules or automated weight selection strategies?
3. Could you ablate joint modeling more directly by training a separate predictor and a planner (with matched capacity) and report closed-loop performance and latency differences?
4. What are the precise latency numbers (ms/plan) and hardware specs for your 20 Hz claim, and how does runtime scale with number of denoising steps and number of neighbors M?
5. How robust is guidance to prediction errors of neighboring agents, especially in reactive simulation where agent behaviors change? Any failure modes when the guidance contradicts predicted future interactions?
6. Do you observe mode collapse or reduced diversity when using low-temperature sampling? How many stochastic samples (if any) are needed to realize the multi-modal benefits in practice?
7. Could you comment on potential integration with a certified safety filter (e.g., short-horizon verifiable shield) and how it would interact with or reduce the need for guidance?
8. Will you release the code and trained weights alongside the dataset to facilitate reproducibility?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents a well-executed and practically relevant application of diffusion models to closed-loop autonomous driving, with careful architectural choices and a compelling demonstration of training-free, composable guidance for safety and personalization. The experimental results on nuPlan (including reactive settings and the Test14-hard split) and a separate delivery-vehicle dataset provide convincing support for the core claims, and the ablations shed light on key design decisions. While the methodology is an incremental evolution rather than a fundamentally new generative formulation, its end-to-end quality and the thorough planning-focused evaluation make it a strong contribution. The main areas for improvement are a deeper quantitative assessment of the guidance mechanism, clearer analysis of the specific gains from joint modeling, and broader contextualization with closely related joint diffusion planning works (e.g., JPPD). Overall, I recommend acceptance; the work will be of significant interest to the ICLR community working on generative decision-making, autonomous driving, and control with diffusion models.
